label: Cancer Survivor MCED (Enriched Population)

sources.name: PATHFINDER / PATHFINDER 2 (GRAIL)
sources.note: MCED performance and PPV in screening populations; use for diagnostic-yield and PPV anchors when reasoning about enrichment.

sources.name: SEER high-mortality cancer incidence data
sources.note: Background incidence and survival by stage for pancreatic, ovarian, liver, and lung cancers. Use for prevalence/effect-size reasoning, labeled as background.

sources.name: Surveillance guidelines for cancer survivors (NCCN/ASCO)
sources.note: Standard-of-care surveillance against which an MCED arm would be compared. Use to define the control arm.

knownFacts: Population: cancer survivors with established risk factors — a higher-prevalence, enriched group versus the general screening population.
Intervention: methylation-based MCED test in addition to standard surveillance.
Target: 12 high-mortality cancer types including pancreatic, ovarian, liver, and lung cancer.
Rationale: enriching for higher baseline risk raises pre-test probability, which raises PPV for a given test sensitivity/specificity.
quantContext: PPV rises with disease prevalence at fixed sensitivity/specificity; an enriched survivor population can materially raise realized PPV relative to a general screening cohort.
Second primary cancers and recurrence are meaningfully more common in survivors than de novo cancers in the general population, raising event rates and statistical power.
Focusing on high-mortality cancers where early-stage detection changes management concentrates the clinically meaningful stage-shift signal.
demoCaveats: Survivor surveillance introduces lead-time and length-time bias considerations that must be addressed in the design.
Do not present specific PPV or sensitivity numbers as measured for this exact population unless sourced — label as estimates.
Distinguishing recurrence/second-primary from de novo cancer is a real classification challenge to flag, not to wave away.
